questionText: The masses, $X$ grams, of apples from an orchard are modelled by $X \sim N(160, 12^2)$. Find the probability that a randomly chosen apple has a mass between $150$ g and $175$ g, giving your answer to $3$ significant figures.

steps.description: State the distribution and the probability required
steps.explanation: The masses are normal with mean $\mu = 160$ and standard deviation $\sigma = 12$ (the $12^2$ is the variance). We want the chance an apple lands in the band between $150$ g and $175$ g, which is $P(150 < X < 175)$.

To use the standard normal we measure each boundary in standard deviations from the mean. Here $150$ is below the mean, so its $z$-value is negative: it sits $0.8333$ standard deviations to the left of $160$.
Doing the same for the upper boundary, $175$ is above the mean, so its standardised value is positive: it lies $1.25$ standard deviations to the right of $160$.

steps.description: Rewrite the probability in terms of $Z$
steps.explanation: Because standardising preserves order, the apple lands between $150$ and $175$ exactly when the standardised value $Z$ lands between the two $z$-values. This turns the problem into one about $Z \sim N(0,1)$.

steps.description: Express as a difference of $\Phi$ values
steps.explanation: The area of a band under the curve is the area up to the right edge minus the area up to the left edge, where $\Phi(z) = P(Z < z)$ is the cumulative area to the left.
From the calculator's normal CDF, about $89.44\%$ of all values lie below $z = 1.25$, i.e. below a mass of $175$ g.
Similarly about $20.23\%$ of values lie below $z = -0.8333$, i.e. below $150$ g. This portion must be removed because it falls outside our band.

steps.description: Subtract to find the band probability
steps.explanation: Removing the left-hand area from the area up to the right boundary leaves precisely the area of the band between $150$ g and $175$ g.

steps.description: State the answer to 3 significant figures
steps.explanation: Rounding gives the required probability. It is comfortably above a half, which is reasonable since the band straddles the mean and covers the most likely masses.
